sentences.text: That is correct.
But I learned English very quickly anyway.
But while I'm on the subject, while I'm still in Liverpool living with the Rainfords, I'd just like to say that years later, actually, when I asked why they decided to take me in, Mr.
Rainford said to me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew I couldn't save the world.
I knew I couldn't stop war from starting.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I knew I could save one child.
And as Hitler-- as Britain and France had broken their pledge to Czechoslovakia, and us, the Jews, were in the most danger, I decided it should be a Czech Jewish child.
And the final choice was made by Dorothy, because they got from the Czech Refugee Trust composites of pictures of five people, five children with details of their background.
And the choice was left to her.
And she chose me because I had blue eyes, fair hair, and a sad smile, she said.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So anyway, we-- both Dorothy and I were evacuated three days before war started.
Actually, my sister was staying with me then.
She came to stay for a week, which was wonderful.
Was that the first time you saw her?
Oh, yes.
Well, we'd only been, been in England a matter of weeks.
And oh, it was so wonderful to see her.
I never left her alone.
I remember we slept in the same bed.
And I remember even when she was in the toilet I stood outside the door so that I wouldn't waste time and talk to her even then.
Dorothy and I were evacuated to another family.
Incidentally, the Rainfords were a very devout Methodist family, and so were the Campions, to whom Dorothy and I went next.
And, and they lived in Southport, which is a little town, safe town quite a long way away from Liverpool, but on the same coast.
Auntie Marjorie, as I called the lady, ruled the household.
She was a very bossy lady, but an excellent cook and an excellent housewife, which Mommy Rainford wasn't.
And, and she was very proud that she housed not only an evacuee, but a refugee.
And she was very proud of this.
Anyway, I started developing very quickly, once I came to England.
And I didn't shoot up so, so much upwards, but I developed hips and a bust.
And all these beautiful clothes Mother had made for me certainly did not fit.
And as clothes were rationed by then, Auntie Marjorie sold mine on the black market.
But she did make me clothes from her daughter's that she had grown out of.
She had a grown-up daughter.
And she was a superb seamstress.
So, you know, I, I was well, well clad.
And she was very good to me and also taught me a lot of, a lot of English.
In fact, she used to do her baking with a Czech-English dictionary in front of her.
And I kept asking, what's this, you know?
What's that?
And, and I never let, let the subject alone.
They had a little dog, and I taught the little dog more Czech than I taught the grown-ups.
Because the little dog called Jill understood in the end all the Czech commands.
But Auntie Marjorie wanted the whole world to know what a good woman she was and how well she looked after her refugees.
And she used to have ladies of the church come to tea every week.
And whenever I came to-- from school, she'd make me stand on a stool and she'd say, now, this poor little refugee.
If it wasn't for me, she'd stand here naked.
And if it wasn't for me, she'd be hungry, you know, and all this.
And I resented it very much.
And I wished-- I thought of, if only these people could see my beautiful mother, could see the clothes, type of clothes she bought me and the wonderful food that came out of her kitchen.
And always before meals prayers were being said.
And one day Auntie Marjorie said, Vera, why don't you say a prayer in Czech?
And I didn't hesitate.
And I said, dear God, can't you stop this woman being so bossy and such a showoff?
Amen.
And I said this prayer for the whole year or year and a half I was with them.
It made me feel much better.
Did you hear from your parents during this period?
I was just going to come to that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Once war started, letters ceased.
At first, we got just one or two through Holland.
And then when Holland fell, you know, the contact was gone.
And they were very sort of stiff and stifled little notes just saying they were well and they were thinking of us.
But I was with the Campions only about two weeks.
Or four weeks I think it was, actually.
And I developed scarlet fever.
Now, scarlet fever in those days was a very serious illness.
And I was rushed to the isolation hospital.
The way Auntie Marjorie looked, I thought I must be dying.
And I felt like it, I must say.
And I was very frightened.
But Dorothy's cheerful face appeared by the door of the ambulance before they closed it.
And she said to me, [NON-ENGLISH], which means au revoir, see you.
So I thought, well, I can't be dying after all.
And when I was in the isolation hospital, Mrs.
Campion obviously contacted Mommy Rainford.
And Mommy Rainford came immediately, didn't even take her-- stop to take her apron off.
And they both came to this hospital to see me.
They weren't allowed inside.
And by then I was already turning somersaults in my bed.
But of course, even the little notes we were able to send via Holland then to my parents stopped because I wasn't allowed to write.
And I was terribly worried, and they were terribly worried at not hearing from me.
So the first thing I did when I came out of hospital was to sit down.
And then I know I wrote in desperation in my diary, what can I say?
I'm not allowed to speak the truth.
I'm not allowed to mention I'm in England.
You know, it was so difficult for an 11-year-old kid, and to find the words, how much she misses them, and yet not to make them sad.
But very soon, the letters stopped all together.
And as I said earlier, after that, it was my diary and the stars and the sun that I turned to, and also my sis.
We wrote almost daily.
My parents wrote to me daily at first until war started.
Every single day a letter came.
And it was a wonderful bond, you know, which for many children-- I mean, there were about 669 children came on these transports from Prague.
And I, I've met hundreds of them.
But not one parent had such a thoughtful idea as those diaries and the contacts with the sun and the stars.
It really-- I think it preserved my sanity and my optimism and my faith, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't stay with the, with Auntie Marjorie terribly long because the Rainfords felt they were responsible for me.
And also Dorothy, who was a very outspoken young lady, clashed with Auntie Marjorie no end.
And when I went into the isolation hospital, she couldn't face a whole month of Auntie Marjorie on her own without me.
And she went home, pleading that she also had to be in quarantine because she was in contact with me.
And she never, ever returned.
And as Liverpool was being extensively bombed, the Rainfords rented a house in Ainsdale, which was also part of Southport.
And then we could be together again.
Not immediately, because it was about six months before they, they moved out.
And then, then I went to live with them.
And then [SIGHS] after Dunkirk it was when many Czech solders fled, and airmen fled together with the president in exile, President Benes, from France to Britain.
And the government in exile was set up in London.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And lots of their families came as well.
Then I sort of felt, I shall have Czech soldiers fighting at the side of the British, you know, for our freedom.
And it seemed a very important [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] role that they had to play.
And that first Christmas after Dunkirk, 1940, 1940, I wrote to President Benes.
And I expressed my faith in him that he would lead us into a free Czechoslovakia.
And I got a little visiting card back from him in acknowledgement.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I heard, months later, that there was going to be a concert given by the Czech forces in Liverpool, and that President Benes and his wife are going to be the guest of honor.
And I nagged the Rainfords to let me attend.
And they said, we can't because of the bombardment, because of the air raids.
And I said, listen, if Precedent Benes isn't afraid, surely you won't keep me away.
And so Mr.
Rainford said OK.
He gave in.
I'll take you.
We'll go.
I'd have to look in my diary.
I think it was-- 19-- the beginning of 1941, I think it was.
Yes, the beginning of 1941.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was a wonderful night for me, to hear Czech music and to see all these men I owed my life to and to see President Benes, whom to me was sort of a very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] special figure, you know, sitting there in the box, made it a really memorable night.
And halfway through the concert, during the interval, armed with a visiting card which I took with me, I marched to the box.
And there were two Czech soldiers on guard there.
And I presented them, I presented them with, with a visiting card.
And I said, would you please tell President Benes that Vera Diamant is here to see him and would like to see him.
He looked at this little kid standing there, but nevertheless amused, they went in and came out and said, please go in.

And to my amazement the president not only remembered me, but remembered the words I put on my card to him and introduced me to his wife saying, this young lady sent me a lovely little letter last Christmas.
And he asked me to stay with him for the rest of the program.
And he told-- asked me if I was happy, if was looked after properly, and, and what I missed.
And I said to him, well, of course, my parents most of all, and being in Czechoslovakia.

But otherwise, I do miss not having Czech education, and particularly as I didn't get into a grammar school, not because of my English, but I couldn't master the maths in, in a month and a half, which, after which I had to take my 11 Plus.
So when I told him how I missed not being in a Czech school and not having Czech children to play with and me and my friends, he said, but I can change all that.
You know, there is a school here in Britain just for such children as you are.
And I tell you what, I'll arrange it myself through my office.
Well, I walked home on stars that night.
I-- my feet never touched the ground.
And I kept talking to the stars.
I kept saying to them, Mommy, Daddy, I hope you know.
I hope you are aware of this.
If only I could write.
If only I could shout and tell you that I'm going, actually, to a Czech school.
And Mr.
Rainford or Daddy Rainford was pleased for me, but a little bit sad that they'd be losing me.
And I said, but you won't be losing me, because I'm going to come back every holiday.
So a fortnight later, I found myself in Whitchurch in Shropshire where there was this old mansion which was a home to the Czech children.
About 100 and odd of us there were there then.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Walking up those stairs, seeing photographs on the wall of the first president, Tomas Masaryk and President Benes, and, you know, and the British king.
It was all there.
And hearing Czech spoken around me, oh, I, I thought I was in heaven.
Only my parents were missing.
I settled down so quickly.
The teachers couldn't get over the fact that I had not lost any of my Czech.
That was thanks to this lady from Sudetenland, of course.
In fact, my Czech was even better than when I was-- than when I came to England.
And so of course, I had no problems settling down to my bilingual education.
And I found myself surrounded by many children who also came on the transports, who also lived in constant hope and constant fear, not knowing what was happening to their beloved families.
There were, of course, children there whose parents were with them in Britain.
And they weren't all Jews.
But I would think about 75% of us were Jewish.
And out of that, about 40% had come on the transports.
We had bilingual education.
Well, most of them were seconded for special service from the army.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some were here as nurses, you know.
And some came here perhaps as parents of some of the children.
There were one or two.
Others were given to us or granted to us by the British Council.
They helped us a lot, also with equipment.
They were very, very kind.
And we had a Scottish teacher who studied and then taught at Prague University, for instance.
And he spoke most abominable Czech.
I would have preferred his English with a Scottish accent.
But he prided himself as being one of the few who learned and mastered Czech, you know, to some degree.
Lots of the teachers we had were really too good to teach us grammar school children because they were of university caliber.
They were professors in their, in their civil life.
But you see, the school had a dual purpose.
It was used by members of the forces to take their matriculation during their leave.
And of course, they had to have their matriculation to, to, be made into a-- for there to be an officer.
So, so for their advancement, they studied and, and came to the school.
It was actually very strange to see these men, particularly the airmen, who perhaps fought in the Battle of Britain and were so brave.
And there they were trembling at the knees because they were taking an exam, you know.

[LAUGHS] And I also remember because food was so hard to come by-- meat particularly-- and we all gave up our weekly rations for these men who were our heroes, who were fighting for our freedom, you know, so that they could have a good feed, because we had a superb, superb cook, who could make a feast out of almost anything.
Excuse me.
Very little, because you see, there was a, a point of dispute before we were accepted on the transports.
But after-- in fact, before the Germans marched in, but after Sudetenland was taken over, our parents had the whole family christened-- or baptized, I should say.
This was purely hoping that we wouldn't be persecuted if it came to persecution.
I mean, lots of Jewish families did.
And it was a purely a safeguard.
But I could not make my English families understand this.
They felt it meant that my parents wanted me to carry on being educated in, in a Christian way.
And as-- when I was with them, I went to a Methodist church.
I went to a Methodist Sunday school, and the same when I was with Auntie Marjorie.
They, being my guardians, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when they had to fill in a form, you see, put me down as a, as a Methodist, of Church of Czechoslovakia, which was of that group.
And though I protested, the, the school felt, as I was in the care of the Rainfords, they had to abide by their wishes.
But though I didn't-- we had a rabbi at school, and he considered me one of his.
And I-- well, I mean, to me it didn't matter.
We were all refugees.
And I didn't, as such, go to many religious classes.
And-- but I, I didn't have Jewish instruction.
I really didn't.
You had very little Jewish instruction in my life, you know, because of this.
But I never considered myself anything but a Jew.
But of course, at the age of 12 or 13, you can't dictate, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you, you can't dictate into which group you should go.
And obviously, my guardian, guardians' wishes had to be adhered to.
Well, my sister was in a Church of England school, boarding school, a very posh school.
She had the advantage of having a first-class education right from the start, but the disadvantage of not having a loving family like I did who actually chose her.
She made friends very quickly, particularly with two girls, and spent her holidays with them.
And they also had me during holidays as well so that we could be together as I used to come to the Rainfords, and I used to come-- or to the Campions when I was with there first.
And you know, I used to come and join her.
And in fact, before I went, ever went into the Czech school, I joined her in her boarding school, which was like something out of a fairy tale, beautiful buildings.
They even had their own lake.
And I remember it was January.
It was in the winter.
And we were skating on the lake, you know.
And of course, being a Czech, I was, I was brought up on skating and skiing.
And, and to have my sister there and all these lovely girls in their uniform, and-- oh, I hated leaving.
I spent a fortnight there, and I wished I could stay with her.
On the other hand, of course, once I got to the Czech school, then I realized what happiness in exile meant, because I was surrounded by kids, as I said, who shared my predicament.
My teachers, who knew what it was, what the war meant for children like myself, they were more like caring relatives.
We were like a large, extended family.
And the ties we forged there during those three and a half years have remained stronger, you know, throughout the years.
And they are here to this day, where we-- most of us had lost all our families, and we are each other's families.
And that was a wonderful blessing.
And the more people I talk to who came on Kindertransports and were orphaned, the more I realize how lucky I was and what my sister actually missed.
And she realized it when she joined me there on a few occasions.
She wished she could have gone.
But by then she was too old.
Well, she was that much older.
And again, she, she read and wrote, you know, her diaries, and wrote to me always in Czech.
It's not as perfect as mine, because I had Czech education also after the war.
But it's, it's pretty good.
But she became very religious, and not in a Jewish way.
I mean, she had to go again to, to church, you know, with the school and with the families.
But you see, what happened-- and again, I've learned this from lots of the children, like my best friend in Prague-- went to a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] first, a very devout Christian family.
And her older sister, who was even older than my sister, managed to get her away.
But then she ended up with a very highly Orthodox family.
And, and she hated that equally.
Both were very foreign to her.
But she said the one thing she didn't mind was going to synagogue or church because she felt her parents needed help.
And the only way she could help them was by praying.
And again, like me, it didn't matter where she prayed.
But the more she prayed, the more likely she would be to help.
And one can understand this type of a feeling.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Where was I?
I was in the Czech school.

Well, we, we went on very happily, eventually moving at the beginning of 1943 to a big hotel in Llanwrytd Wells, which is now in Powys in Central Wales, which-- and Llanwrytd Wells was a village in those days, though now it is the smallest town of England and Wales.
And the people there looked upon us with a mixture of curiosity and resentment, you know, this huge lot of Czechs invading their little village.
And we wondered how to break the ice.
And then somebody hit upon a brilliant idea, one of the teachers.
And we organized a concert.
And of course, there's no better way than music to get into a Welsh heart.
And they all came, you know, from-- down to the last man, they crowded into our hall.
And we danced our national dances and sang Czech songs and English songs.
And then at the end of the concept, we the Czech children stood up and sang the national, the Welsh national anthem in Welsh.
And there wasn't a dry eye in the audience.
And from then on, they were all our friends.
And all their doors were open to us.
And they couldn't do enough for us.
In fact, we have the citizenship of Llanwrytd Wells.
We are allowed to drive sheep and dig for peat, [LAUGHS] you know?
And to this day, we hold our reunions there.
And there is an exchange of the local school with a school in Czechoslovakia, you know.
So the love story goes to this day.
We, we were very lucky and very happy in, in, in that little town.
Why did you move there?
Well, because more and more children found out about the school and wanted to join.
And there was not enough room in Hinton Hall.
Plus it was falling to bits.
It was a very old mansion.
And balconies fell down.
And then one day the ceiling in the dining room crashed.
And luckily the only casualty was a young boy whose finger was broken.
And after that, the headmaster really thought he'd have to make the government provide us with something more suitable.
All right.
